content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 7, 1990


Robert R. Wellington
City Attorney
City of Marina
505 Abrego Street
Monterey, CA 93940

Re: Your Request Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-249

Dear Mr. Wellington:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding application of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ to the Marina City Council. Since your question is general in nature and you have not named a specific elected official on whose behalf you have requested this advice, we can only provide the following informal response to your question pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

QUESTION

Does the public generally exception applicable to small jurisdictions apply to the City of Marina, where the city boundaries include 30,113 persons, but the city's population independent of the federal areas, is only 16,733?

CONCLUSION

Regulation 18703.1 would not be available for the City Council of Marina because the jurisdiction of the city council is the entire city, including the population near Fort Ord that has been annexed to the city.


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-249
Page 2

FACTS

The City of Marina is situated west of the United States Army
base at Fort Ord. Several years ago, Marina annexed a portion of
Ford Ord, adding 13,380 persons to the city's existing population
of 16,733.

The City of Marina does not provide municipal services to the
area, such as police or fire protection, and does not collect
property taxes from the area. In addition, the area is not
subject to the city's transient occupancy tax, property transfer
tax, planning and land use/building inspection fees and the sales
and use tax on retail sales. However, the city does receive tax
benefits based on population due to the inclusion of the area.
Further, the residents of that area do vote in city elections.

Because the city covers a small geographic area, it is not
unusual to have more than one councilmember disqualified due to
conflicts of interest. You have asked whether the public gener-
ally exception applicable to small jurisdictions, Regulation
18703.1, applies to the City of Marina, where the city boundaries
include 30,113 persons, but the city's population independent of
the federal areas is only 16,733.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 specifies that an official has
a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on any real property in which the public official has a
direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or more. (Section
87103(b).)

Regulation 18703.1 provides that under certain specified
circumstances the effect of a governmental decision on a public
official's principal residence is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally in small jurisdictions. Subdivi-
sion (a) of the regulation provides that the exception is only
available if:

The public official's agency has jurisdiction
over a population of 25,000 or less, covering a
geographic area of ten square miles or less.

Our File No. I-90-249
Page 3

You have asked how subsection (a) of Regulation 18703.13/
(copy enclosed) is interpreted with respect to the term "jurisdic-
tion." Section 82035 of the Act defines "jurisdiction" as:

....the state with respect to a state agency and,
with respect to a local government agency, the
region, county, city, district or other
geographical area in which it has jurisdiction.
The jurisdiction of a member of a regional coastal
zone conservation commission shall be the permit
area in which the regional commission has jurisdic-
tion. Real property shall be deemed to be "within
the jurisdiction" with respect to a local govern-
ment agency if the property or any part of it is
located within or not more than two miles outside
the boundaries of the jurisdiction or within two
miles of any land owned or used by the local
government agency.

Funk and Wagnalls Standard College Dictionary (1974) defines
"jurisdiction" as the lawful "right to exercise official authori-
ty, whether executive, legislative or judicial." Or, in the
alternative the "territory within...[which] such lawful authority
may be lawfully exercised." Under your facts, while the city
council's authority with respect to the area in question is
limited in comparison with the council's authority over the rest
of the city, the council, nevertheless, has some authority and
official duties with respect to the region. This is further sup-
ported by the fact that the persons residing in that area vote in
city elections.

Further, it would appear that not only are the persons resid-
ing in that area constituents of the city council, but they may
also be impacted by city council decisions. In 1985 in In re
Legan (9 FPPC Ops. 1, copy enclosed) the Commission stated:

The "public" includes the entire jurisdiction
for the agency in question, particularly where an
elective body is being considered, because all of
the County's residents are constituents of the
various members of the Board of Supervisors and
many reside in the various incorporated cities
within the County. They are all impacted in one
way or another by the County's land-use decisions,
even if not directly subject to the County's land-
use jurisdiction. We see no reason to alter our

3/         Regulation 18703.1 was adopted by the Commission in December
1989. The regulation will be submitted to the Office of
Administrative Law in the near future. However, in the interim,
the Commission will be advising on the basis of the language of
the new regulation.

Our File No. I-90-249
Page 4

interpretation that the residents, and businesses
and property owners of the County of Santa Clara
are the "public" for purposes of decisions being
made by the Board of Supervisors.

In re Legan, supra, at 12.

Thus, persons in cities within a county are part of the
jurisdiction of the county board of supervisors despite the fact
that the cities are not directly subject to the board of
supervisor's authority. We believe this rationale applies here as
well.

Finally, the clear intent of the regulation in question was
to consider the jurisdiction of a city council, to be the city
boundaries for purposes of subdivision (a). In the memorandum in
support of the regulation (copy enclosed) we said:

Subdivision (a) offers two alternatives to
specify the size of the jurisdiction for purposes
of the exception:
-- a population ranging in size of less than
15,000 to 25,000; and
-- a geographic area ranging in size of less
than five to fifteen square miles.

According to the League of California Cities,
there are approximately 143 cities with populations
of 15,000 or less covering an area of five square
miles or less. Another 36 cities cover an area of
fifteen square miles or less, with populations of
15,000 or less. Since there are a total of 453
cities in California, those with a population of
15,000 or less covering 15 square miles or less
comprise approximately 40% of all cities in
California. We have no information on other agencies with small jurisdictions, such as special
districts, school districts or other similar public
entities.

No special use of the term "jurisdiction" was suggested in
the memorandum, nor does it appear that the information provided
by the League of Cities determined the population of city other
than by considering the city boundaries. Thus, it appears from
this language that the term "jurisdiction" was meant to reflect
the population in the cities' boundaries. Moreover, it is a
fundamental rule of statutory construction that an exception to a
general rule is to be narrowly construed. (Julius Goldman's Egg
City v. Air Pollution Control Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116
Cal.App. 3d 741; Valdez v. Federal Mut. Ins. Co. (1969) 272
Cal.App. 2d 223.) Regulation 18703.1 is an exception to the

Our File No. I-90-249
Page 5


general rules requiring disqualification on the basis of an
official's economic interests. As such, the interpretation of the
regulation which confines application of the exception is
preferred. Accordingly, the entire population of the city,
including persons residing at Port Ord, is the relevant population
for purposes of determining whether Regulation 18703.1 applies.

Consequently, we conclude that the jurisdiction of Marina is
the entire city, including the population near Fort Ord that was
annexed to the city. Thus, Regulation 18703.1 would not be avail-
able for the City Council of Marina.4/

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JWW:plh

Enclosures


4/ Absent facts to the contrary, we would also conclude that the
city's planning commission has jurisdiction that is coterminous
with the city council. Consequently, the exception would
similarly not be available for the Marina Planning Commission.
